story:
Henry returns from the hospital to his childhood house and, as always, fumbles at the door, feeling the key skitter away among its identical siblings as if the place resented him for leaving or for coming back at all. Inside, the house has stopped being a home and become a dozen empty rooms gathered around a lot of silence. The hall looks as it did when he was a boy—old telephone on a stack of directories, framed sea and landscape prints—except for a plastic calendar with sliding numbers, frozen on January 15, the day his father went into hospital.

He could walk the place blind, guided by memory alone, yet something feels wrong. “Hello,” he says. No answer, but the word echoes as if someone might be listening. He climbs the stairs one creaking step at a time, feeling watched, like a child daring himself past the neighborhood’s haunted house. He tells himself, in the voice of a reasonable adult, that the reaction is absurd: the house was empty when he left and stayed that way while he sat by his father’s bedside.

At the top he turns into his old bedroom, reclaimed two months earlier by the pull of his father’s illness. The curtains are drawn, the air musty. He strips off his shirt and steps into the bathroom, steams the mirror—and a noise sounds behind him. In the glass his eyes widen. It isn’t one of the house’s familiar groans. He rushes out onto the landing, a cold prickle on his shoulders. No one is there. He goes into his father’s room, untouched since the hospital, a dusty museum. Then a stealthy scuffling comes from below the landing. He runs back—and something shoots up the stairs past his head. He ducks, arms up. A gust of air carries a musty, unclean taint. A small brown bird hovers against the ceiling, its wings a blur.

The intruder alarms him more than any imagined burglar. He doesn’t ask how it got in; he only wants it out. He charges along the landing, shouting and windmilling his arms. The bird bangs against the door frame and darts into the bedroom. He follows, hears his voice rise thin and hysterical, like a child on the wrong side of a game, and forces himself calm, listening to his own loud heart. He spots the bird at ceiling height in a shaft of sun, black pebble eyes fixed on him. Rage flares. He lunges. The chase becomes a shabby cartoon—he blunders, it flutters—repeating until both of them retreat, panting, to their corners like foolish wrestlers. At the window the bird’s eyes reflect him back—two tiny images of a man made wild by panic and anger—and that hurts more than anything.

This time his anger has purpose. He herds the bird onto the landing and into the bathroom, slams the door. Dust sifts down as it batters the door’s top edge. He flings open the small window to a blast of cold air, snatches a towel, sets the light fixture swinging, raises more dust. The bird skids along the sill. He traps it, misses once, then grabs a greasy bundle of feathers wrapped around a frenzied heart and shoves it through the open window. He slams the casement and turns the lock. For a heartbeat the bird hangs there, unblinking, then beats its wings and is gone.

Henry stands, listening to the cymbal crash of his pulse. Already the whole episode feels unreal, a dream dropping away. Downstairs, the old hall telephone, silent since his father went into hospital on January 15, begins to ring.
